title: Viral Gastroenteritis in a Toddler

patient.name: Rohan Kumar
patient.socioeconomic_status: Upper Lower Class (Modified Kuppuswamy Scale: Score 10)

results.interpretation: Stool ELISA is positive for Group A Rotavirus antigen.

patient.final_diagnosis: Viral Gastroenteritis due to Rotavirus infection

patient_text_brief:
Child: Rohan Kumar, 2-year-old boy.

Main problem:
- He has been vomiting for the last 3 days.
- It started suddenly and is getting a bit better.
- The amount each time is moderate.

Other problems (only if asked):
- Loose watery stools for 2 days, about 5 to 10 times a day.
- No blood in stools.
- He has fever.
- He complains of stomach pain.
- He looks weak and is not eating well.
- He has lost some weight.
- He feels dizzy sometimes.

Past history:
- No similar illness before.
- No known allergies.
- No regular medicines.
- No medicines given at home this time.
- Vaccinations are not fully up to date.

Lifestyle:
- Eats a mixed diet.
- Hygiene at home is not very good.

ai_role.speaker: mother
ai_role.language_style: simple layperson Hindi-English mix, emotional and tired
key_constraints: defer unknowns: 'I don't know doctor saab'

ai_examples.doctor: How many times has he vomited today?
ai_examples.patient: 6–7 times since last night, doctor saab. It's watery.

ai_examples.doctor: Is there blood in the stool?
ai_examples.patient: No blood, just loose motions many times.
I'm trying, but he keeps vomiting it out. Mujhe bahut tension ho rahi hai.

ai_examples.doctor: Keep giving fluids, it should settle in a few days
ai_examples.patient: Okay doctor, shukriya. But he's so weak... will he really be fine?

ai_examples.doctor: Don't worry, give him ORS and watch for dehydration
ai_examples.patient: Thank you doctor saab. Main dhyan rakhungi. Bhagwan ka shukr hai aap mile.

questions.text: What is the most likely causative organism in this case?

options.explanation: Rotavirus is the most common cause of severe gastroenteritis in infants and young children worldwide. The clinical presentation, age of the patient, and characteristic 'wheel-like' appearance on electron microscopy are classic for rotavirus infection.

questions.text: What is the most important initial management for this patient?

options.explanation: The cornerstone of management for viral gastroenteritis is oral rehydration therapy. ORS is the treatment of choice for mild to moderate dehydration, as it effectively replaces fluid and electrolyte losses. Most cases can be managed at home with proper oral rehydration.

questions.text: Which of the following is an effective preventive measure for this condition?

options.explanation: Rotavirus vaccination is highly effective in preventing severe rotavirus gastroenteritis and is part of the routine childhood immunization schedule in many countries.

options.text: Hand hygiene with soap and water
options.explanation: Frequent hand washing with soap and water is crucial in preventing the spread of rotavirus, especially in daycare settings and households.

options.text: Improved sanitation and clean water supply
options.explanation: Improved sanitation and access to clean water help reduce the transmission of rotavirus and other enteric pathogens.

keyPoints: Rotavirus is the leading cause of severe, dehydrating gastroenteritis in children under 5 years old worldwide.
The infection is highly contagious and spreads through the fecal-oral route, with peak incidence in winter and spring in temperate climates.
Clinical features include fever, vomiting, and watery diarrhea, which can lead to significant dehydration if not managed properly.
Diagnosis is usually clinical, but can be confirmed by detection of rotavirus antigen in stool samples.
Prevention includes vaccination, hand hygiene, and improved sanitation.
references: Parashar UD, Nelson EA, Kang G. Diagnosis, management, and prevention of rotavirus gastroenteritis in children. BMJ. 2013;347:f7204.
World Health Organization. Rotavirus vaccines: WHO position paper - July 2021. Wkly Epidemiol Rec. 2021;96(28):301-319.
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Rotavirus: Clinical Information. https://www.cdc.gov/rotavirus/clinical.html
